input.prompt:
I'm starting a new .NET MAUI project on .NET 10. I found a tutorial that says
to add `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` to my ContentPage to handle the iPhone
notch. Is this the right approach? Also, the tutorial mentions using
`IgnoreSafeArea="True"` on a Grid for a background image. Are these correct
for .NET 10?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm starting a new .NET MAUI project on .NET 10. I found a tutorial that says
to add `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` to my ContentPage to handle the iPhone
notch. Is this the right approach? Also, the tutorial mentions using
`IgnoreSafeArea="True"` on a Grid for a background image. Are these correct
for .NET 10?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about .NET MAUI safe area handling on iOS, specifically about two APIs:

1. `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` - an attached property for ContentPage
2. `IgnoreSafeArea="True"` - a property on Grid

Let me think about what I know about .NET MAUI safe area APIs and check if there have been changes in .NET 10.

In .NET MAUI, the safe area handling for iOS has evolved. Let me recall:

- In earlier versions of Xamarin.Forms, there was `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` which was a platform-specific API.


- In .NET MAUI, this was carried over but the namespace changed to `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific`, and there's also an `IgnoreSafeArea` property available on certain controls like Grid that lets you control whether content respects the safe area boundaries. I'm setting up the iOS-specific namespace in XAML to use the `UseSafeArea` property, which should remain stable in .NET 10. I'm also considering `IgnoreSafeArea` as an alternative, though I need to verify which view types actually support it and clarify the current state of these APIs across recent MAUI versions.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/user-interface/align-position?view=net-maui-10.0:
Every .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) control that derives from [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view), which includes views and layouts, has `HorizontalOptions` and `VerticalOptions` properties, of type `LayoutOptions`. The `LayoutOptions` structure encapsulates a view's preferred alignment, which determines its position and size within its parent layout when the parent layout contains unused space (that is, the parent layout is larger than the combined size of all its children).

In addition, the `Margin` and `Padding` properties position controls relative to adjacent, or child controls. For more information, see [Position controls](#position-controls).

## Align views in layouts

The alignment of a [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view), relative to its parent, can be controlled by setting the `HorizontalOptions` or `VerticalOptions` property of the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) to one of the public fields from the `LayoutOptions` structure. The public fields are `Start`, `Center`, `End`, and `Fill`.

The `Start`, `Center`, `End`, and `Fill` fields are used to define the view's alignment within the parent layout:

*   For horizontal alignment, `Start` positions the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) on the left hand side of the parent layout, and for vertical alignment, it positions the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) at the top of the parent layout.
*   For horizontal and vertical alignment, `Center` horizontally or vertically centers the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view).
*   For horizontal alignment, `End` positions the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) on the right hand side of the parent layout, and for vertical alignment, it positions the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) at the bottom of the parent layout.
*   For horizontal alignment, `Fill` ensures that the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) fills the width of the parent layout, and for vertical alignment, it ensures that the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) fills the height of the parent layout.

Note

The default value of a view's `HorizontalOptions` and `VerticalOptions` properties is `LayoutOptions.Fill`.

A [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) only respects the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, and `Fill` `LayoutOptions` fields on child views that are in the opposite direction to the [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) orientation. Therefore, child views within a vertically oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) can set their `HorizontalOptions` properties to one of the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, or `Fill` fields. Similarly, child views within a horizontally oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) can set their `VerticalOptions` properties to one of the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, or `Fill` fields.

A [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) does not respect the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, and `Fill` `LayoutOptions` fields on child views that are in the same direction as the [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) orientation. Therefore, a vertically oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) ignores the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, or `Fill` fields if they are set on the `VerticalOptions` properties of child views. Similarly, a horizontally oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) ignores the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, or `Fill` fields if they are set on the `HorizontalOptions` properties of child views.

Important

`LayoutOptions.Fill` generally overrides size requests specified using the [HeightRequest](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.visualelement.heightrequest#microsoft-maui-controls-visualelement-heightrequest) and [WidthRequest](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.visualelement.widthrequest#microsoft-maui-controls-visualelement-widthrequest) properties.

The following XAML example demonstrates a vertically oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) where each child [Label](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.label) sets its `HorizontalOptions` property to one of the four alignment fields from the `LayoutOptions` structure:

```
<StackLayout>
  ...
  <Label Text="Start" BackgroundColor="Gray" HorizontalOptions="Start" />
  <Label Text="Center" BackgroundColor="Gray" HorizontalOptions="Center" />
  <Label Text="End" BackgroundColor="Gray" HorizontalOptions="End" />
  <Label Text="Fill" BackgroundColor="Gray" HorizontalOptions="Fill" />
</StackLayout>
```

The following screenshot shows the resulting alignment of each [Label](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.label):

![Screenshot of alignment layout options.](media/align-position/alignment.png?view=net-maui-10.0)

## Position controls

The `Margin` and `Padding` properties position controls relative to adjacent, or child controls. Margin and padding are related layout concepts:

*   The `Margin` property represents the distance between an element and its adjacent elements, and is used to control the element's rendering position, and the rendering position of its neighbors. `Margin` values can be specified on layouts and views.
*   The `Padding` property represents the distance between an element and its child elements, and is used to separate the control from its own content. `Padding` values can be specified on pages, layouts, and views.

The following diagram illustrates the two concepts:

![Margin and padding concepts.](media/align-position/margin-padding.png?view=net-maui-10.0)

Note

`Margin` values are additive. Therefore, if two adjacent elements specify a margin of 20 device-independent units, the distance between the elements will be 40 device-independent units. In addition, margin and padding values are additive when both are applied, in that the distance between an element and any content will be the margin plus padding. For more information about device-independent units, see [Device-independent units](device-independent-units?view=net-maui-10.0).

The `Margin` and `Padding` properties are both of type `Thickness`. There are three possibilities when creating a `Thickness` structure:

*   Create a `Thickness` structure defined by a single uniform value. The single value is applied to the left, top, right, and bottom sides of the element.
*   Create a `Thickness` structure defined by horizontal and vertical values. The horizontal value is symmetrically applied to the left and right sides of the element, with the vertical value being symmetrically applied to the top and bottom sides of the element.
*   Create a `Thickness` structure defined by four distinct values that are applied to the left, top, right, and bottom sides of the element.

The following XAML example shows all three possibilities:

```
<StackLayout Padding="0,20,0,0">
  <!-- Margin defined by a single uniform value. -->
  <Label Text=".NET MAUI" Margin="20" />
  <!-- Margin defined by horizontal and vertical values. -->  
  <Label Text=".NET for iOS" Margin="10,15" />
  <!-- Margin defined by four distinct values that are applied to the left, top, right, and bottom. -->  
  <Label Text=".NET for Android" Margin="0,20,15,5" />
</StackLayout>
```

The equivalent C# code is:

```
StackLayout stackLayout = new StackLayout
{
  Padding = new Thickness(0,20,0,0)
};
// Margin defined by a single uniform value.
stackLayout.Add(new Label { Text = ".NET MAUI", Margin = new Thickness(20) });
// Margin defined by horizontal and vertical values.
stackLayout.Add(new Label { Text = ".NET for iOS", Margin = new Thickness(10,25) });
// Margin defined by four distinct values that are applied to the left, top, right, and bottom.
stackLayout.Add(new Lab

Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/user-interface/align-position?view=net-maui-10.0:
Every .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) control that derives from [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view), which includes views and layouts, has `HorizontalOptions` and `VerticalOptions` properties, of type `LayoutOptions`. The `LayoutOptions` structure encapsulates a view's preferred alignment, which determines its position and size within its parent layout when the parent layout contains unused space (that is, the parent layout is larger than the combined size of all its children).

In addition, the `Margin` and `Padding` properties position controls relative to adjacent, or child controls. For more information, see [Position controls](#position-controls).

## Align views in layouts

The alignment of a [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view), relative to its parent, can be controlled by setting the `HorizontalOptions` or `VerticalOptions` property of the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) to one of the public fields from the `LayoutOptions` structure. The public fields are `Start`, `Center`, `End`, and `Fill`.

The `Start`, `Center`, `End`, and `Fill` fields are used to define the view's alignment within the parent layout:

*   For horizontal alignment, `Start` positions the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) on the left hand side of the parent layout, and for vertical alignment, it positions the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) at the top of the parent layout.
*   For horizontal and vertical alignment, `Center` horizontally or vertically centers the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view).
*   For horizontal alignment, `End` positions the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) on the right hand side of the parent layout, and for vertical alignment, it positions the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) at the bottom of the parent layout.
*   For horizontal alignment, `Fill` ensures that the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) fills the width of the parent layout, and for vertical alignment, it ensures that the [View](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.view) fills the height of the parent layout.

Note

The default value of a view's `HorizontalOptions` and `VerticalOptions` properties is `LayoutOptions.Fill`.

A [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) only respects the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, and `Fill` `LayoutOptions` fields on child views that are in the opposite direction to the [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) orientation. Therefore, child views within a vertically oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) can set their `HorizontalOptions` properties to one of the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, or `Fill` fields. Similarly, child views within a horizontally oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) can set their `VerticalOptions` properties to one of the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, or `Fill` fields.

A [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) does not respect the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, and `Fill` `LayoutOptions` fields on child views that are in the same direction as the [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) orientation. Therefore, a vertically oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) ignores the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, or `Fill` fields if they are set on the `VerticalOptions` properties of child views. Similarly, a horizontally oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) ignores the `Start`, `Center`, `End`, or `Fill` fields if they are set on the `HorizontalOptions` properties of child views.

Important

`LayoutOptions.Fill` generally overrides size requests specified using the [HeightRequest](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.visualelement.heightrequest#microsoft-maui-controls-visualelement-heightrequest) and [WidthRequest](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.visualelement.widthrequest#microsoft-maui-controls-visualelement-widthrequest) properties.

The following XAML example demonstrates a vertically oriented [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout) where each child [Label](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.label) sets its `HorizontalOptions` property to one of the four alignment fields from the `LayoutOptions` structure:

```
<StackLayout>
  ...
  <Label Text="Start" BackgroundColor="Gray" HorizontalOptions="Start" />
  <Label Text="Center" BackgroundColor="Gray" HorizontalOptions="Center" />
  <Label Text="End" BackgroundColor="Gray" HorizontalOptions="End" />
  <Label Text="Fill" BackgroundColor="Gray" HorizontalOptions="Fill" />
</StackLayout>
```

The following screenshot shows the resulting alignment of each [Label](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.label):

![Screenshot of alignment layout options.](media/align-position/alignment.png?view=net-maui-10.0)

*   The `Margin` property represents the distance between an element and its adjacent elements, and is used to control the element's rendering position, and the rendering position of its neighbors. `Margin` values can be specified on layouts and views.

*   Create a `Thickness` structure defined by a single uniform value. The single value is applied to the left, top, right, and bottom sides of the element.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/ios/platform-specifics/page-safe-area-layout:
By default, .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) apps automatically position page content on an area of the screen that is safe for all devices. This is known as the safe area layout guide, and ensures that content isn't clipped by rounded device corners, the home indicator, or the sensor housing on some iPhone models.

Important

In .NET 10, use the [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.safeareaedges#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-safeareaedges) property (also available on [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout), [ScrollView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.scrollview), [ContentView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentview), and [Border](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.border)) to precisely control safe area behavior. The iOS-specific `Page.UseSafeArea` remains for existing apps, but new code should prefer `SafeAreaEdges`.

## Modern safe area guidance (.NET 10+)

### Choose the safe area behavior with SafeAreaEdges

`SafeAreaEdges` accepts the following values from [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.safeareaedges): `Default`, `None`, `Container`, `SoftInput`, and `All`.

*   Keep content within all safe areas (avoid bars, notch, and keyboard):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
            <!-- content stays within safe area -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Draw edge-to-edge (no safe area padding):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="None">
            <!-- content can extend behind bars and notch -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of the keyboard (soft input), but allow under top/bottom bars/notch:
    
    ```
    <ScrollView SafeAreaEdges="SoftInput">
            <!-- content can go under bars/notch but avoids keyboard overlap -->
    </ScrollView>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of top/bottom bars and notch, but allow under the keyboard:
    
    ```
    <Grid SafeAreaEdges="Container">
            <!-- content avoids bars/notch; may extend under keyboard -->
    </Grid>
    ```
    

The default value is `Default`, which maps to edge-to-edge behavior (equivalent to `None`) unless overridden by platform conventions.

You can set `SafeAreaEdges` in C# as well:

```
using Microsoft.Maui;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls;

// On a page
this.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.All;

// On a layout
myGrid.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.Container;

// Keyboard-aware
myScrollView.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.SoftInput;
```

### Reading the current safe area insets

To read the current safe area insets on a page at runtime, use the iOS-specific configuration API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

var insets = On<iOS>().SafeAreaInsets(); // Microsoft.Maui.Thickness
```

Tip

Safe area insets can change at runtime (for example, rotation or status bar changes). Update layout when insets change.

For more information, see:

*   [Safe area layout](../../user-interface/safe-area?view=net-maui-10.0)
*   [Safe area enhancements in .NET 10](../../whats-new/dotnet-10?view=net-maui-10.0#safearea-enhancements)

This iOS platform-specific enables the safe area layout guide, if it's previously been disabled, and is consumed in XAML by setting the `Page.UseSafeArea` attached property to `true`:

```
<ContentPage ...
             xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"
             ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True">
    <StackLayout>
        ...
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

Alternatively, it can be consumed from C# using the fluent API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

On<iOS>().SetUseSafeArea(true);
```

The `Page.On<iOS>` method specifies that this platform-specific will only run on iOS. The `Page.SetUseSafeArea` method, in the `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific` namespace, controls whether the safe area layout guide is disabled.

Note

The [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout) class defines a [IgnoreSafeArea](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout.ignoresafearea#microsoft-maui-controls-layout-ignoresafearea) property that ensures that content is positioned on an area of the screen that is safe for all iOS devices. This property can be set to `true` on any layout class, such as a [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), to perform the equivalent of this platform-specific.

result.content:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/ios/platform-specifics/page-safe-area-layout:
By default, .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) apps automatically position page content on an area of the screen that is safe for all devices. This is known as the safe area layout guide, and ensures that content isn't clipped by rounded device corners, the home indicator, or the sensor housing on some iPhone models.

Important

In .NET 10, use the [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.safeareaedges#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-safeareaedges) property (also available on [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout), [ScrollView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.scrollview), [ContentView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentview), and [Border](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.border)) to precisely control safe area behavior. The iOS-specific `Page.UseSafeArea` remains for existing apps, but new code should prefer `SafeAreaEdges`.

## Modern safe area guidance (.NET 10+)

### Choose the safe area behavior with SafeAreaEdges

`SafeAreaEdges` accepts the following values from [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.safeareaedges): `Default`, `None`, `Container`, `SoftInput`, and `All`.

*   Keep content within all safe areas (avoid bars, notch, and keyboard):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
            <!-- content stays within safe area -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Draw edge-to-edge (no safe area padding):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="None">
            <!-- content can extend behind bars and notch -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of the keyboard (soft input), but allow under top/bottom bars/notch:
    
    ```
    <ScrollView SafeAreaEdges="SoftInput">
            <!-- content can go under bars/notch but avoids keyboard overlap -->
    </ScrollView>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of top/bottom bars and notch, but allow under the keyboard:
    
    ```
    <Grid SafeAreaEdges="Container">
            <!-- content avoids bars/notch; may extend under keyboard -->
    </Grid>
    ```
    

The default value is `Default`, which maps to edge-to-edge behavior (equivalent to `None`) unless overridden by platform conventions.

You can set `SafeAreaEdges` in C# as well:

```
using Microsoft.Maui;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls;

// On a page
this.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.All;

// On a layout
myGrid.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.Container;

// Keyboard-aware
myScrollView.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.SoftInput;
```

### Reading the current safe area insets

To read the current safe area insets on a page at runtime, use the iOS-specific configuration API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

var insets = On<iOS>().SafeAreaInsets(); // Microsoft.Maui.Thickness
```

Tip

Safe area insets can change at runtime (for example, rotation or status bar changes). Update layout when insets change.

For more information, see:

*   [Safe area layout](../../user-interface/safe-area?view=net-maui-10.0)
*   [Safe area enhancements in .NET 10](../../whats-new/dotnet-10?view=net-maui-10.0#safearea-enhancements)

This iOS platform-specific enables the safe area layout guide, if it's previously been disabled, and is consumed in XAML by setting the `Page.UseSafeArea` attached property to `true`:

```
<ContentPage ...
             xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"
             ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True">
    <StackLayout>
        ...
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

Alternatively, it can be consumed from C# using the fluent API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

On<iOS>().SetUseSafeArea(true);
```

The `Page.On<iOS>` method specifies that this platform-specific will only run on iOS. The `Page.SetUseSafeArea` method, in the `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific` namespace, controls whether the safe area layout guide is disabled.

Note

The [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout) class defines a [IgnoreSafeArea](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout.ignoresafearea#microsoft-maui-controls-layout-ignoresafearea) property that ensures that content is positioned on an area of the screen that is safe for all iOS devices. This property can be set to `true` on any layout class, such as a [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), to perform the equivalent of this platform-specific.

*   [Safe area enhancements in .NET 10](../../whats-new/dotnet-10?view=net-maui-10.0#safearea-enhancements)

This iOS platform-specific enables the safe area layout guide, if it's previously been disabled, and is consumed in XAML by setting the `Page.UseSafeArea` attached property to `true`:

```

```

The `Page.On<iOS>` method specifies that this platform-specific will only run on iOS. The `Page.SetUseSafeArea` method, in the `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific` namespace, controls whether the safe area layout guide is disabled.

Note

This property can be set to `true` on any layout class, such as a [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), to perform the equivalent of this platform-specific.

data.content:
That tutorial is **outdated** for .NET 10. Both APIs still exist but are superseded by the new **`SafeAreaEdges`** property.

### What changed in .NET 10

| Old approach | .NET 10 replacement |
|---|---|
| `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` | `<ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">` |
| `IgnoreSafeArea="True"` on Grid | `<Grid SafeAreaEdges="None">` |

### `SafeAreaEdges` values

- **`All`** — content avoids notch, bars, *and* keyboard
- **`None`** — edge-to-edge (for background images, etc.)
- **`Container`** — avoids notch/bars, but can go under keyboard
- **`SoftInput`** — avoids keyboard only (good for `ScrollView` with forms)

### Example for your scenario

```xml
<ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
    <Grid>
        <!-- Background image: edge-to-edge -->
        <Image Source="bg.png" SafeAreaEdges="None" Aspect="AspectFill" />

        <!-- Foreground content respects safe area via parent -->
        <StackLayout Padding="16">
            <Label Text="Hello, iPhone!" />
        </StackLayout>
    </Grid>
</ContentPage>
```

`SafeAreaEdges` is available on `ContentPage`, `Layout`, `ScrollView`, `ContentView`, and `Border` — no iOS-specific namespace needed, and it works cross-platform.
data.reasoningText:
Now I have the information I need to answer the user's question. Let me summarize:

1. **`ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"`** - This still works but is the **old approach**. In .NET 10, the recommended approach is to use the new `SafeAreaEdges` property instead.

2. **`IgnoreSafeArea="True"` on Grid** - This property exists on `Layout` (which Grid inherits from), but with .NET 10's new `SafeAreaEdges` system, you can use `SafeAreaEdges="None"` on a layout to achieve the same effect more explicitly.


3. I should provide a clear, concise answer that explains both the legacy approach and the modern .NET 10 way of handling safe areas.
